{ CloudCore Solutions }
01 / 12

Manga DataHub

Migration & modernisation of Manga's data architecture to the cloud

A cloud-native AWS data lakehouse response to the RFP, built for governed data, real-time operations, and ML-ready growth.

IE UniversityMBD-EN2025Cloud Analytics

Andrea Alarc?n Valles ? Mateus Carneiro ? Tina Jannasch ? Ricardo Li?vano Pedroza ? Luka Tcheishvili ? Nicklas Urban

{ Why now }
02 / 12

Manga is growing, but the data stack cannot keep up.

The gap is not strategy. It is architecture.

?280MAnnual revenue
+3.5%Growth, below market
1.2%Online sales YoY
?2?Infra cost vs competitors
Daily cron-job ETL leaves managers deciding on yesterday's data.
Siloed pipelines were built by separate teams without shared standards.
Excel is the BI layer, so there is no real self-service or real-time operating view.
GDPR posture is unclear after a prior security incident.
{ Proposal }
03 / 12

The Manga DataHub: one governed AWS lakehouse.

Three design pillars convert fragmented legacy data into a compounding platform.

Unify

One governed platform for every source, replacing siloed pipelines and manual Excel.

Accelerate

Time-to-insight from 24 hours to sub-second for live inventory, sales, and risk signals.

Enable

ML that is impossible today: recommendations, dynamic pricing, and demand forecasting.

Built on open-source tooling: Airflow, Spark, and open Parquet. No vendor lock-in.

{ Section 2 }
05 / 12

High-Level Architecture: technology-agnostic.

A clean separation of sources, ingestion, lakehouse storage, processing, consumption, and governance.

Separation

Storage decoupled from compute

Modularity

Failures do not cascade

Elasticity

Auto-scales for Fashion-Week spikes

Open standards

Parquet, Airflow, Spark

{ Section 3 }
06 / 12

Low-Level Architecture: mapped to AWS.

The same architecture, now expressed through managed AWS services and open formats.

Ingestion

Kinesis ? Glue ? Lambda ? DMS

Storage

S3 lakehouse ? Redshift ? DynamoDB

Governance

Glue Catalog ? MWAA ? Lake Formation ? Macie

Consumption

QuickSight ? SageMaker ? Athena ? API Gateway

{ Section 4 ? R6 }
08 / 12

Projected ~57% lower infrastructure cost.

Replacing fixed colocation with AWS pay-per-use creates annual savings that matter to the CFO.

?1.30MColocation / year
?0.56MAWS / year
?57%Cost reduction
?0.74MSaved every year
{ Delivery }
09 / 12

Nine-month delivery in three phases.

A controlled-risk roadmap that reaches production in month 7 and handover by month 9.

Months 1?3

Phase 1 ? Foundation

AWS accounts, Terraform baseline, Dev live, DMS migration, Catalog + MWAA, first batch pipelines.

Months 4?6

Phase 2 ? Build & Validate

Pre-Prod live, Kinesis streaming, Glue DQ + Lake Formation, QuickSight replacing Excel, first ML model.

Months 7?9

Phase 3 ? Cutover & ML

Production live, colocation decommissioned, recommendations + dynamic pricing, security audit, handover.

Key risks mitigated: parallel DMS CDC before cutover ? open-source for lock-in ? DPIA before any personal data.
{ Section 5 }
10 / 12

ML use cases on real data.

The platform turns Manga's operational data into forecasting, personalisation, and merchandising intelligence.

Demand Forecasting

SageMaker DeepAR on sales + weather + events

?30% stockouts ? ?20% overstock

Product Recommendations

Collaborative filtering served at ?5ms via DynamoDB

+12?18% average order value

Sentiment Merchandising

Comprehend NLP on reviews, alerts to buyers

2?3 weeks earlier warning
The same foundation extends to dynamic pricing (+3?7% margin) and return-rate optimisation (?10?15% processing cost).

A platform that keeps compounding value.

The architecture is built to absorb future AI, commerce, partner, and sustainability work without another rebuild.
